titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 24 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Spender briefly mentions Lillian's work with Lady Leinster, Ulster politics, and life at the front.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
24 June My pr Perhaps Just now I
am rather glad of your
meetings with Lady Leitrim,
but I don't want you to
undertake anything which means
less rest now from your work.
You must spare yourself for
your work all you can now. 2 such lovely letters
from you, but your news makes
me anxious. I'm afraid
Sredward has made a great
mistake, either some of the
Unionist leaders have misled
him or there has been a
betrayal somewhere. A nice letter from Craig
but we do not see eye to eye
I'm afraid now. I think we
could have killed home rule &
fear we have only temporised
with it. There is a good deal
of rumbling today and I
was up at the front with
Richardson in his cubby hole
but am not going to him
for a day or 2 yet as
P cannot spare me now! I don't know when you
will get this but hope yesterday's
scribble will explain
a big gap in my letters,
hope I shall get my papers
at any rate. Am still quite busy, but
hope part of my own responsibility
will be off tomor
tomorrow, tho' it does not
mean much. Yes old thing
we need not worry about
the future now we know
each other. Your b w
WB Spender
